name: pypesh
documentation:
![Tests](https://github.com/turczyneq/pypesh/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg) advection diffusion solver ============================== This repository attempts to find a solution to advection diffusion problem $$ 0 = \Delta \phi - \mathrm{Pe} (u \cdot \nabla \phi) $$ with $\phi = 1$ for $z \to \infty$ and $\phi = 0$ on a surface of the sphere and $\mathrm{Pe}$ denoting Peclet number. Final determined value is Sherwood number defined as $$ \mathrm{Sh} = \frac{\Phi}{ 4 \pi D R}$$ Where $D$ is diffusion constant and $\Phi$ is flux falling onto the sphere.

We use two approaches: `scikit-fem` package to handle solving which requires rewriting equations in weak form for smaller peclets and `pychastic` to generate and trace trajcetories of single particles and . # Usage as module Basic usage ```Python python3 -m pypesh --peclet 1000 --ball_radius 0.9 ``` Sample output: ``` Sherwood for given parameters is 12.033892568100546 ``` # Usage as package Basic usage ```Python import pypesh.pesh as psh psh.sherwood(peclet = 10**4, ball_radius = 0.9) ``` For advanced options go to: https://pypesh.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ License ------- Copyright (C) 2024 Radost Waszkiewicz and Jan Turczynowicz. This repository is published under GPL3.0 license. Bibliography ------------ - *Bubbles, Drops and Particles*; R. Clift, J. Grace, M. Weber (1978) - *Electrochemical measurements of mass transfer between a sphere and liquid in motion at high Peclet numbers*; S. Kutateladze, V. Nakoryakov, M. Iskakov (1982) - *Mass and heat transfer from fluid spheres at low Reynolds numbers*; Z. Feng, E. Michaelides (2000) - *Heat transfer from spheres to flowing media*; H. Kramers (1946)

functions.name: pypesh.analytic.sherwood_from_flux
documentation:
sherwood is defined as flux_dimesional/(4 pi D R) = U R^2 flux / (4 pi D R) = flux\*(Pe/4 pi)

# Parameters

flux: float

: Flux passing the ceiling

peclet: float

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

# Returns

float

: sherwood from dimensionless flux

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.analytic as analytic
>>> analytic.sherwood_from_flux(0.1, 1000)
7.957747154594768
```

functions.name: pypesh.analytic.clift_approximation
documentation:
Analytic approximation to numerical solution of Clift et. al.

# Parameters

pe

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

# Returns

float

: Sherwod calculated from Clift et. al. approximation

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.analytic as analytic
>>> analytic.clift_approximation(1000)
6.800655008742168
```

functions.name: pypesh.analytic.our_approximation
documentation:
Our approximation of Sherwood number for `peclet` and `ball_radius`

# Parameters

peclet

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

ball_radius

: Radius of big ball

# Returns

float

: Sherwod calculated our approximation

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.analytic as analytic
>>> analytic.our_approximation(1000, 1)
6.800655008742168
>>> analytic.our_approximation(1000, 0.9)
10.425655008742165
```

functions.name: pypesh.fem._get_mesh
documentation:
Loads mesh defined by mesh_type and caches the mesh to avoid repetative loading.

# Parameters

mesh_type: {'default', 'fine', 'coarse'}

: 'default' for medium peclet, 'fine' for big peclets and 'coarse' for small peclets

# Returns

tuple

: skfem object \<skfem MeshTri1 object>, used by scikit-fem and \<skfem CellBasis(MeshTri1, ElementTriP1) object> boundaries of mesh

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.fem as fem
>>> fem._get_mesh('default')
(<skfem MeshTri1 object>
Number of elements: 90756
Number of vertices: 46225
Number of nodes: 46225
Named boundaries [# facets]: left [972], right [40], top [264], bottom [20], ball [396], <skfem CellBasis(MeshTri1, ElementTriP1) object>
Number of elements: 90756
Number of DOFs: 46225
Size: 19603296 B)
```

functions.name: pypesh.fem.get_mesh
documentation:
Loads mesh adequate to Peclet number

# Parameters

peclet: float

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

# Returns

tuple

: skfem object \<skfem MeshTri1 object> used by scikit-fem, \<skfem CellBasis(MeshTri1, ElementTriP1) object> boundaries of mesh

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.fem as fem
>>> fem.get_mesh(1000)
(<skfem MeshTri1 object>
Number of elements: 90756
Number of vertices: 46225
Number of nodes: 46225
Named boundaries [# facets]: left [972], right [40], top [264], bottom [20], ball [396], <skfem CellBasis(MeshTri1, ElementTriP1) object>
Number of elements: 90756
Number of DOFs: 46225
Size: 19603296 B)
```

functions.name: pypesh.fem.sherwood_fem
documentation:
sherwood is defined as flux_dimesional/(4 pi D R) = U R^2 flux / (4 pi D R) = flux\*(Pe/4 pi)

# Parameters

peclet: float

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

ball_radius

: Radius of the big ball.

# Returns

float

: Sherwood calcualted for selected peclet and ball radius using fem approach.

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.fem as fem
>>> fem.sherwood_fem(10000, 0.9)
54.93467214954524
```

functions.name: pypesh.generate_mesh.gen_mesh
documentation:
Generates mesh used then by sciki-fem to solve advection-diffusion in Stokes Flow.

# Parameters

mesh: float, optional

: Density of triangles in most interesting region

far mesh: float, optional

: Density of triangles in region far away from axis, ceiling and sphere

cell_size: float, optional

: Scales cell size, keeping ratio of walls to ceiling

width: float, optional

: Width of cell

ceiling: float, optional

: Distance from 0 to upper boundary of cell

floor: float, optional

: Distance from 0 to lower boundary of cell

save: Bool, optional

: Default False, if True than mesh will be saved in /meshes/

show

: Default False, if True mesh is shown

# Returns

\<skfem MeshTri1 object>

: skfem object, used by scikit-fem

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.generate_mesh as msh
>>> msh.gen_mesh()
Transforming over 1000 vertices to C_CONTIGUOUS.
Transforming over 1000 elements to C_CONTIGUOUS.
<skfem MeshTri1 object>
Number of elements: 90756
Number of vertices: 46225
Number of nodes: 46225
Named boundaries [# facets]: left [972], right [40], top [264], bottom [20], ball [396]
```

functions.name: pypesh.pesh.all_sherwood
documentation:
Calculates the sherwood number using clift approximation, fem approach and trajectories approach.

# Parameters

peclet

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

ball_radius

: Radius of the big ball.

mesh_out

: For trajectories, amount of samples outside the region of highest slope

mesh_jump

: For trajectories, amount of samples in the region of highest slope

trials

: For trajectories, number of trajectories per position, uncertainty of propability estimation is sqrt(trials)/trials.

floor_h

: For trajectories, initial depth for simulation

spread: int, optional

: For trajectories, how far in sqrt(1/peclet), mesh_out will reach

# Returns

tuple

: float - Clift et. al., float - our approximation, float - fem approach, float - trajectories approach

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.pesh as pesh
>>> pesh.all_sherwood(1000, 0.9)
(6.800655008742168, 10.425655008742165, np.float64(12.033892568100546), 14.194223139015682)
```

functions.name: pypesh.pesh._sherwood
documentation:
Interpolate the sherwood number for given peclet and ball_radius.

# Parameters

peclet

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

ball_radius

: Radius of the big ball.

# Returns

float

: Sherwood number for given peclet and ball_radius

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.pesh as pesh
>>> pesh._sherwood(1000, 0.9)
12.033892568100546
```

functions.name: pypesh.pesh.sherwood_heatmap
documentation:
Interpolate the sherwood number for given peclet and ball_radius and returns a function.

# Parameters

# Returns

function

: Gives sherwood number for given peclet and ball_radius

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.pesh as pesh
>>> import numpy as np
>>> interp = pesh.sherwood_heatmap()
>>> to_call = np.array([1000,0.9])
>>> interp(to_call)
array([12.03389257])
```

functions.name: pypesh.pesh.main
documentation: Calculates the sherwood number for given peclet and ball_radius basing on recomended approach. (WARNING) trajectories approach takes long to calculate and default mesh and trajectories are not supporting accurate values, recomended increasing trials, mesh_jump and mesh_out.
Peclet number defined as R u / D.
For trajectories, amount of samples outside the region of highest slope.
For trajectories, amount of samples in the region of highest slope.
For trajectories, number of trajectories per position, uncertainty of propability estimation is sqrt(trials)/trials.
For trajectories, initial depth for simulation.
For trajectories, how far in sqrt(1/peclet), mesh_out will reach.
Sherwood number for given peclet and ball_radius
# TODO: only peclet is poor approach, should be on basis of pe*\beta**2

functions.name: pypesh.trajectories._construct_initial_trials_at_x
documentation:
Constructs initial conditions for slected x position at the bottom

# Parameters

floor_h

: Initial depth for simulation

x_postition

: Position to generate initial conditions

trials

: Number of trajectories per initial condition.

# Returns

jnp.array

: Array [x_position, 0, -floor_h] trials times

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.trajectories as traj
>>> traj.construct_initial_trials_at_x(2, 5, 3)
Array([[ 2.,  0., -5.],
    [ 2.,  0., -5.],
    [ 2.,  0., -5.]], dtype=float32
```

functions.name: pypesh.trajectories._diffusion_function
documentation:
Simple noice operator describing diffusion

Compare:
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fokker%E2%80%93Planck_equation>

# Parameters

peclet

: dimesnionless number defined as U R / D

# Returns

callable

: jnp.array, Identity matrix times drift coeficient, dimensionalized sqrt(2 D)\*dW, where D - diffusion, dW - wiener process

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.trajectories as traj
>>> fun = traj.diffusion_function(100)
>>> fun([0,0,1])
Array([[0.14142136, 0.        , 0.        ],
    [0.        , 0.14142136, 0.        ],
    [0.        , 0.        , 0.14142136]], dtype=float32)
```

functions.name: pypesh.trajectories.simulate_trajectory
documentation:
Simulates trajectories starting from initial conditions, affected by noise and moved via drift.

# Parameters

derift

: Function describing drift term of the equation, should return np.array of length `dimension`.

noise

: Function describing noise term of the equation, should return np.array of size `(dimension,noiseterms)`.

initial

: Array of positions where to start simulating

t_max

: Time of calculation

whole_trajectory

: Deafult False, if True than also returns whole trajectory (Warning expensive in memory)

seed: int

: Default 0, if diferent than 0, different random seed is used for SDE.

# Returns

dict

: `ball_hit` - jnp.array() of 1 and 0, if 1 trajectory within radius 1, 0 miss
  `roof_hit` - jnp.array() of 1 and 0, if 1 trajectory at the end above height 2, 0 miss
  `something_hit` - jnp.array() of 1 and 0, union of `ball_hit` and `roof_hit`

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.trajectories as traj
>>> import pypesh.stokes_flow as sf
>>> def drift(q):
...     return sf.stokes_around_sphere_jnp(q, 0.9)
>>> noise = traj.diffusion_function(100)
>>> traj.simulate_trajectory(drift, noise, init, 20)
{'ball_hit': Array([ True, False, False], dtype=bool), 'roof_hit': Array([False,  True,  True], dtype=bool), 'something_hit': Array([ True,  True,  True], dtype=bool)}
```

functions.name: pypesh.trajectories.draw_trajectory_at_x
documentation:
Generate trajectories of particles in a simulation for certain x position amd returns whole trajectory.

# Parameters

x_postition

: Radius where probability is evaluated (simulation initiation point)

peclet

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

ball_radius

: Radius of the big ball.

trials

: Default 5, Number of trajectories.

floor_h

: Default 5, Initial depth for simulation

t_max

: Default 5, time of simulation

# Returns

dict

: `ball_hit` - jnp.array() of 1 and 0, if 1 trajectory within radius 1, 0 miss
  `roof_hit` - jnp.array() of 1 and 0, if 1 trajectory at the end above height 2, 0 miss
  `something_hit` - jnp.array() of 1 and 0, union of `ball_hit` and `roof_hit`
  `trajectories` - jnp.array() `trials` by `100*t_max` by 3 with `x(t), y(t), z(t)` positions for each trial.

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.trajectories as traj
>>> traj.draw_trajectory_at_x(0.1, 1000, 0.9, trials = 1, t_max = .1)
{'ball_hit': Array([False], dtype=bool), 'roof_hit': Array([False], dtype=bool), 'something_hit': Array([False], dtype=bool), 'trajectories': Array([[[ 1.01513535e-01,  1.41892245e-03, -4.98940468e+00],
        [ 1.03897616e-01,  9.31997492e-04, -4.97806931e+00],
        [ 1.04288198e-01, -1.80142978e-03, -4.97903204e+00],
        [ 9.97019112e-02, -3.12771578e-03, -4.96664095e+00],
        [ 1.02413967e-01, -2.95094796e-03, -4.96111631e+00],
        [ 1.01300426e-01,  6.96127070e-04, -4.94909143e+00],
        [ 1.03424884e-01, -6.30148593e-03, -4.94255972e+00],
        [ 1.03292465e-01, -5.54783177e-03, -4.93507099e+00],
        [ 9.94927734e-02, -4.31211060e-03, -4.92812681e+00],
        [ 9.83759165e-02, -7.30469078e-03, -4.92086792e+00]]],      dtype=float32)}
>>> traj.draw_trajectory_at_x(0.1, 1000, 0.9, trials = 2, t_max = .05)
{'ball_hit': Array([False, False], dtype=bool), 'roof_hit': Array([False, False], dtype=bool), 'something_hit': Array([False, False], dtype=bool), 'trajectories': Array([[[ 9.8736316e-02, -7.5448438e-04, -4.9963560e+00],
        [ 9.6689783e-02, -4.3136943e-03, -4.9961877e+00],
        [ 9.7639665e-02, -5.3856885e-03, -4.9852118e+00],
        [ 9.8702230e-02, -7.6030511e-03, -4.9785647e+00],
        [ 9.1684863e-02,  7.3614251e-04, -4.9669151e+00]],
```

> \[[ 1.0136999e-01, -1.1048245e-02, -4.9944925e+00],
>
> : [ 1.0074968e-01, -3.2547791e-03, -4.9900651e+00],
>   [ 1.0723757e-01, -6.1367834e-03, -4.9799914e+00],
>   [ 1.1393108e-01, -1.7973720e-03, -4.9786887e+00],
>   [ 1.1445464e-01, -7.9164971e-03, -4.9739923e+00]\]\], dtype=float32)}

functions.name: pypesh.trajectories.hitting_propability_at_x
documentation:
Generate trajectories of particles in a simulation for certain x position. Than calculates the propability of hitting for this position.

# Parameters

x_postition

: Radius where probability is evaluated (simulation initiation point)

peclet

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

ball_radius

: Radius of the big ball.

trials

: Number of trajectories.

floor_h

: Initial depth for simulation.

parition

: Default 1, if to expensive in RAM partition into 'partition' parts.

t_max: float, optional

: Default 40.0, select time of simmulation

# Returns

float

: value of propability

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.trajectories as traj
>>> traj.hitting_propability_at_x(0.1, 10**4, 0.9)
0.91
```

functions.name: pypesh.trajectories._weighted_trapezoidal
documentation:
Calculate integral of function with weight, expresion to integrate is: 2\*pi\*r\*vz(r)\*function(r). On each step assume function(r) is a\*r + b and then integrate.

# Parameters

function

: Keys are xargs and values are yargs of function to integrate

ball_radius

: Radius of the big ball.

z

: Height where integration is taking place

# Returns

float

: value of integral

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.trajectories as traj
>>> import numpy as np
>>> dictionary = {x: np.cos(x) for x in np.linspace(0, np.pi/2, 10)}
>>> traj.weighted_trapezoidal(dictionary, 1, 5)
2.5493506321093182
```

functions.name: pypesh.trajectories.sherwood_trajectories
documentation:
Calculates the distribution of probability of hitting as a function of radius, at depth floor_h. Addaptive sampling is implemented to ensure effective calculation. Position of greatest slope is assumed to be at streamline that pass position [1, 0, 0], then spread is scaled as sqrt(1/peclet) sim sqrt(D). Then integrates with weigth and finds the Sherwood number

# Parameters

peclet

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

ball_radius

: Radius of the big ball.

mesh_out

: Amount of samples outside the region of highest slope

mesh_jump

: Amount of samples in the region of highest slope

trials

: Number of trajectories per position, uncertainty of propability estimation is sqrt(trials)/trials.

floor_h

: Initial depth for simulation

spread: int, optional

: How far in sqrt(1/peclet), mesh_out will reach

parition

: Default 1, if to expensive in RAM partition into 'partition' parts.

t_max: float, optional

: Default 40.0, select time of simmulation

# Returns

tuple

: float - estimation of resulting sherwood number (flux/advective_flux), array - Radius samples, array - Probability values ordered as radius samples

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.trajectories as traj
>>> traj.sherwood_trajectories(10**6, 0.9)
(3714.4541773411393, array([0.10064565, 0.11064565, 0.12064565, 0.13064565, 0.13464565,
   0.13864565, 0.14264565, 0.14664565, 0.15064565, 0.16064565,
   0.17064565, 0.18064565]), array([1.  , 1.  , 1.  , 1.  , 0.94, 0.76, 0.39, 0.19, 0.07, 0.  , 0.  ,
   0.  ], dtype=float32))
```

functions.name: pypesh.visualisation.draw_cross_section_fem
documentation:
Draws cross section of hitting probability at selected height using scikit-fem.

# Parameters

peclet

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

ball_radius

: Radius of the big ball.

downstream_distance

: Default 5, downstream distance in ball radius where distribution is plotted

show

: Default False, if True plot is shown

denstity

: Default 200, how many points in result

maximal_radius

: Default 1, maximal radius to place in result

# Returns

np.array

: `density` by 2 array with location probability pairs.

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.visualisation as visual
>>> visual.draw_cross_section_fem(1000, 0.9, density = 10)
array([[ 0.00000000e+00,  7.96836968e-01],
    [ 1.11111111e-01,  6.95108509e-01],
    [ 2.22222222e-01,  4.52912665e-01],
    [ 3.33333333e-01,  2.11422004e-01],
    [ 4.44444444e-01,  6.74661539e-02],
    [ 5.55555556e-01,  1.42087363e-02],
    [ 6.66666667e-01,  1.95186128e-03],
    [ 7.77777778e-01,  1.57053994e-04],
    [ 8.88888889e-01,  5.67654858e-06],
    [ 1.00000000e+00, -2.40005303e-07]])
```

functions.name: pypesh.visualisation.draw_cross_section_traj
documentation:
Draws cross section of hitting probability at selected height using pychastic.

# Parameters

peclet

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

ball_radius

: Radius of the big ball.

downstream_distance

: Default 5, downstream distance in ball radius where distribution is plotted

show

: Default False, if True plot is shown

denstity

: Default 10, how many points in result

maximal_radius

: Default 1, maximal radius to place in result

mesh_out

: Amount of samples outside the region of highest slope

mesh_jump

: Amount of samples in the region of highest slope

spread: int, optional

: How far in sqrt(1/peclet), mesh_out will reach

trials

: Default 200, Number of trajectories.

# Returns

np.array

: `density` by 2 array with location probability pairs.

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.visualisation as visual
>>> visual.draw_cross_section_traj(1000, 0.9)
array([[0.        , 0.8       ],
    [0.09137468, 0.675     ],
    [0.18274937, 0.505     ],
    [0.27412405, 0.285     ],
    [0.36549873, 0.155     ],
    [0.45687341, 0.07      ],
    [0.93121506, 0.        ],
    [1.40555671, 0.        ]])
```

functions.name: pypesh.visualisation.visualise_trajectories
documentation:
Draws trajectories simulated by pychastic.

# Parameters

peclet

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

ball_radius

: Radius of the big ball.

positions: dict

: Keys: position where simulate, Values: how many times

t_max

: Default 20, time of simulation

downstream_distance

: Default 5, downstream distance in ball radius where distribution is plotted

show

: Default False, if True plot is shown

# Returns

list

: list of outcomes from pychastic for each position

# Example

```pycon
>>> visual.visualise_trajectories(1000, 0.9, {0.1: 2, 0.2: 1}, t_max=0.05)
[{'ball_hit': Array([False, False], dtype=bool), 'roof_hit': Array([False, False], dtype=bool), 'something_hit': Array([False, False], dtype=bool), 'trajectories': Array([[[ 9.8736316e-02, -7.5448438e-04, -4.9963560e+00],
        [ 9.6689783e-02, -4.3136943e-03, -4.9961877e+00],
        [ 9.7639665e-02, -5.3856885e-03, -4.9852118e+00],
        [ 9.8702230e-02, -7.6030511e-03, -4.9785647e+00],
        [ 9.1684863e-02,  7.3614251e-04, -4.9669151e+00]],
```

> \[[ 1.0136999e-01, -1.1048245e-02, -4.9944925e+00],
>
> : [ 1.0074968e-01, -3.2547791e-03, -4.9900651e+00],
>   [ 1.0723757e-01, -6.1367834e-03, -4.9799914e+00],
>   [ 1.1393108e-01, -1.7973720e-03, -4.9786887e+00],
>   [ 1.1445464e-01, -7.9164971e-03, -4.9739923e+00]\]\], dtype=float32)}, {'ball_hit': Array([False], dtype=bool), 'roof_hit': Array([False], dtype=bool), 'something_hit': Array([False], dtype=bool), 'trajectories': Array(\[\[[ 2.0366783e-01, -2.2119847e-03, -5.0009050e+00],
>   [ 2.0216057e-01, -7.5238775e-03, -4.9919944e+00],
>   [ 2.0585570e-01, -5.9383763e-03, -4.9826236e+00],
>   [ 2.1121313e-01, -7.7165901e-03, -4.9811354e+00],
>   [ 2.1137653e-01, -7.2739599e-03, -4.9675722e+00]\]\], dtype=float32)}\]

functions.name: pypesh.visualisation.visualise_trajectories_with_streamplot
documentation:
Draws trajectories simulated by pychastic.

# Parameters

peclet

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

ball_radius

: Radius of the big ball.

positions: dict

: Keys: position where simulate, Values: how many times

t_max

: Default 20, time of simulation

downstream_distance

: Default 5, downstream distance in ball radius where distribution is plotted

show

: Default False, if True plot is shown

# Returns

list

: list of outcomes from pychastic for each position

# Example

```pycon
>>> visual.visualise_trajectories(1000, 0.9, {0.1: 2, 0.2: 1}, t_max=0.05)
[{'ball_hit': Array([False, False], dtype=bool), 'roof_hit': Array([False, False], dtype=bool), 'something_hit': Array([False, False], dtype=bool), 'trajectories': Array([[[ 9.8736316e-02, -7.5448438e-04, -4.9963560e+00],
        [ 9.6689783e-02, -4.3136943e-03, -4.9961877e+00],
        [ 9.7639665e-02, -5.3856885e-03, -4.9852118e+00],
        [ 9.8702230e-02, -7.6030511e-03, -4.9785647e+00],
        [ 9.1684863e-02,  7.3614251e-04, -4.9669151e+00]],
```

> \[[ 1.0136999e-01, -1.1048245e-02, -4.9944925e+00],
>
> : [ 1.0074968e-01, -3.2547791e-03, -4.9900651e+00],
>   [ 1.0723757e-01, -6.1367834e-03, -4.9799914e+00],

>   [ 1.1445464e-01, -7.9164971e-03, -4.9739923e+00]\]\], dtype=float32)}, {'ball_hit': Array([False], dtype=bool), 'roof_hit': Array([False], dtype=bool), 'something_hit': Array([False], dtype=bool), 'trajectories': Array(\[\[[ 2.0366783e-01, -2.2119847e-03, -5.0009050e+00],
>   [ 2.0216057e-01, -7.5238775e-03, -4.9919944e+00],

functions.name: pypesh.visualisation.draw_distributions_fem
documentation:
Draws distribution of concentration

# Parameters

peclet

: Peclet number defined as R u / D.

ball_radius

: Radius of the big ball.

limits

: minimal radius, maximal radius, minimal height, maximal height

draw_streamline

: Default False, if True adds a streamline for `pe -> \infty`

# Returns

None

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.visualisation as visual
>>> visual.draw_distributions_fem(1000, 0.9)
```

functions.name: pypesh.stokes_flow.stokes_around_sphere_jnp
documentation:
Given location of the tracer find drift velocity -- Stokes flow around sphere of size big_r (stationary) and ambient flow u_inf = [0,0,1].

Location is measured from the centre of the sphere.

Compare:
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stokes%27_law#Transversal_flow_around_a_sphere>

# Parameters

q: jnp.array

: Lenght 3, cartesian position from centre of the sphere.

ball_radius: float

: Radius of ball.

# Returns

jnp.array

: Flow velocity in cartesian coordinates at q

# Example

```pycon
>>> import jax.numpy as jnp
>>> import pypesh.stokes_flow as sf
>>> posjnp = jnp.array([1,1,1])
>>> sf.stokes_around_sphere_jnp(posjnp, 0.9)
Array([-0.0948298, -0.0948298,  0.4803847], dtype=float32, weak_type=True)
```

functions.name: pypesh.stokes_flow.stokes_around_sphere_np
documentation:
Given location of the tracer find drift velocity -- Stokes flow around sphere of size big_r (stationary) and ambient flow u_inf = [0,0,1].

Location is measured from the centre of the sphere.

Compare:
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stokes%27_law#Transversal_flow_around_a_sphere>

# Parameters

q: np.array

: Lenght 3, cartesian position from centre of the sphere.

ball_radius: float

: Radius of ball.

# Returns

np.array

: Flow velocity in cartesian coordinates at q

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.stokes_flow as sf
>>> import numpy as np
>>> posnp = np.array([1,1,1])
>>> sf.stokes_around_sphere_np(posnp, 0.9)
array([-0.09482978, -0.09482978,  0.48038476])
```

functions.name: pypesh.stokes_flow.stokes_around_sphere_explicite
documentation:
Given location of the tracer find drift velocity -- Stokes flow around sphere of size big_r (stationary) and ambient flow u_inf = [0,0,1].

Location is measured from the centre of the sphere.

Compare:
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stokes%27_law#Transversal_flow_around_a_sphere>

# Parameters

r: any

: Radius

z: any

: Height

ball_radius: float

: Radius of ball.

# Returns

tuple

: Velocity in rho direction, Velocity in phi direction, Velocity in z direction

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.stokes_flow as sf
>>> sf.stokes_around_sphere_explicite(1, 1, 0.7)
(-0.14013972519640885, 0, 0.4583120114372806)
```

functions.name: pypesh.stokes_flow.psi
documentation:
Given location of the tracer find streamfunction -- Stokes flow around sphere of size ball_radius (stationary) and ambient flow u_inf = [0,0,1].

Location is measured from the centre in cylindrical coordinates [r, phi, z], z is parallel to ambient flow.

Compare:
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stokes%27_law#Transversal_flow_around_a_sphere>

# Parameters

r: float

: distance from central axis of ball.

z: float

: height above the plane perpendicular to ambient flow, containing centre of sphere

ball_radius: float

: Radius of ball.

# Returns

float

: value of streamline at [r, 0, z]

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.stokes_flow as sf
>>> sf.psi(1, 1, 1)
0.05805826175840782
```

functions.name: pypesh.stokes_flow.streamline_radius
documentation:
Find the radius of streamline at heigh z, that goes through the position [r_start, 0, 0]

Location is measured from the centre in cylindrical coordinates [r, phi, z], z is parallel to ambient flow.

# Parameters

z: float

: height above the plane perpendicular to ambient flow, containing centre of sphere

ball_radius: float

: Radius of ball.

r_start: float

: radius of searched streamline for z = 0

# Returns

float

: radius of streamline that pases [r_start, 0, 0]

# Example

```pycon
>>> import pypesh.stokes_flow as sf
>>> sf.streamline_radius(5, 0.8)
0.2710224007612492
```
